title: Chapter 12: The Ghost
content: The reinforced door had a window, and beyond the window, a hand had pressed against the glass. Jane stood before it now, her pulse a drum in her throat, the corridor's cold air still biting through her clothes. She reached for the handle.
It wasn't locked.

She hesitated. That was wrong. Everything about this corridor — the keypad on the basement door, the concrete, the recycled chill pumping through vents she couldn't see — said security. Said containment. The electronic lock mounted on the door frame blinked a patient red, its mechanism on the outside. Controlled from the corridor. Not to keep people out.
To keep someone in.

But the handle turned from this side. She could open it. She could walk in. Which meant whoever was inside could walk out — if they could get someone on the other side to release the lock, or if they could reach the corridor itself. A cage with a door that only opened from outside was still a cage, even if the bars were silk.
Jane pushed.
The door swung inward on silent hinges, and the room inside was nothing — nothing — like she had expected.
Not a cell. A workshop.

Silk lined the walls, pale gold dupioni pinned from floor to ceiling, the fabric catching the overhead lights and throwing a warm, honeyed glow across every surface. The effect was immediate and disorienting, like stepping from a concrete bunker into a jeweler's case. Her fingers twitched at her side — she could feel the silk from here, the way it would crackle faintly under pressure, the slubs catching light like water over stone.

A long worktable dominated the center of the room, its surface covered in tools of the trade: shears, chalk, pins in ceramic dishes, spools of thread in colors that ranged from ivory to scarlet. Shelves lined one wall, stacked with folded fabric — linen, wool, silk, jersey — organized with a precision that bordered on compulsive. A dress form stood in the corner, half-draped in muslin. Racks of half-finished garments lined the opposite wall, shoulders touching like a silent audience.
It was luxurious by most standards. By any standard, if you didn't notice the details.

No windows. Not one. The recycled air hummed through a single ceiling vent, carrying the chemical edge of fabric treatment — formaldehyde, sizing, the sharp mineral tang of dye fixative — underlaid with something sweeter, older. Starch. The smell of a Turkish bath, Grace Poole had said. Jane tasted metal on her tongue, the cold electric bite of adrenaline dumping into her bloodstream.
This was a beautiful cage.
And there she was.

Standing at the worktable, fabric draped across her forearms, a pair of heavy shears lying beside her left hand. The shears Jane had been hearing for a week — the snip-snip-snip through the floorboards, the sound she'd convinced herself was plumbing, was settling, was anything but what it was: a woman cutting cloth in a room no one was supposed to know existed.
Bertha Mason.

She was not raving. She was not rocking in a corner. She was not the madwoman of Thornfield's whispered mythology — the thing in the attic, the illness, the shame. She was standing at a worktable with fabric in her hands, and her eyes met Jane's across the room with a clarity that was almost physical, a blade turned edge-forward.

Dark hair, hacked short at an angle — not a style, a practicality, the kind of crop you gave yourself with kitchen scissors and a determination not to let it touch your collar. Her skin was brown, warm-toned, the deep amber of someone who had once lived in tropical light and now lived in none. She was tall — taller than Jane by several centimeters — and lean in a way that spoke of years, not of deprivation, but of efficiency. A body maintained. Fuel, not indulgence.
And she was wearing something extraordinary.

A jacket — deconstructed, reconstructed, made from remnants. The collar was raw-edged, the seams exposed and topstitched in contrasting thread. Safety pins stood in for buttons down the front placket, their metal heads catching the light like tiny rivets. One sleeve was silk, the other a matte wool, and the hem was asymmetrical, longer in the back, a silhouette that shouldn't have worked but did, brilliantly, the way a knife works — by being exactly what it is, without apology.
Punk energy in a cage of silk.

Jane stared. The jacket was unlike anything Thornfield produced. Unlike anything in the fashion world, period. It was savage and deliberate and wrong in all the ways that made it right, and it had been made in a windowless room by a woman the world believed was mad.
Bertha's eyes moved over Jane — the worn black sweater, the sensible trousers, the hands still raised from pushing the door. Assessing. Calculating. Then:
"You're the new one."
Not a question. A classification. Jane had been sorted and filed in the time it took to blink.
"I'm the one who found your letters."

The words came out before Jane could shape them, raw and direct, and they hit the room like a stone dropped into still water. Bertha's hands went still on the fabric. Perfectly, absolutely still — the kind of stillness that comes from a body arresting itself against a reflex too dangerous to allow.

For one breath, her composure cracked. Just a flash — a fissure in the iron control, something raw and desperate and starving surfacing in her dark eyes. Jane saw it: the thing Bertha had been holding down for years, the hope she'd beaten into submission because hope was a cruelty when no one was listening.
Then it was gone. The mask reassembled, seam by seam, and Bertha Mason stood straighter.
"Letters?"

Jane's throat was dry. The taste of adrenaline flooded back, metallic and sharp, coating her molars. "The red thread. HELP. NOT CRAZY. Stitched into the seam allowances. Changed out with the hand rotations — every few weeks, a new piece, a new word. Someone in this house has been reading them, or at least passing them on. I thought—" She stopped. Swallowed. "I thought they were letters."

Bertha put down the fabric. Slowly. Deliberately. The way you put down something fragile when your hands have begun to shake and you can't trust them not to destroy it. Her fingers were trembling — a fine, involuntary tremor that she couldn't stop. She gripped the edge of the worktable, knuckles whitening, pressing her palms flat against the wood as if she could anchor herself through sheer force of contact.
"You read them."
Not a question either. A confirmation. A witnessing.
Jane nodded. "I read fabric." Her voice was steadier now, because this part she knew — this part was the only thing she'd ever been sure of. "That's what I do."
Bertha sat down.

Not gracefully. Not with any of the composed elegance her posture had promised. She sat the way a building collapses when the last support gives out — heavily, suddenly, all at once, as if her legs had simply decided they were finished and withdrew their cooperation. The stool scraped against the floor as she caught herself, both palms still pressed flat on the table, arms braced, head bowed.
"Someone finally read them."

Her voice was barely above a whisper. The sound of it — the rawness, the crack at the edges, the way it caught on the word "finally" like a thread snagged on a nail — hit Jane somewhere deeper than logic. This was not performance. This was not manipulation. This was a woman who had been screaming into a void for five years, and the void had just answered back.

Bertha pressed harder into the table, grounding herself. Jane could see the tendons in her wrists standing out, the effort of physical self-regulation, the body trying to keep the mind from flying apart. Then, with the visible labor of someone restarting a machine that hadn't run in years — cranking the engine, feeling the cylinders catch, feeding fuel into a system gone dry with disuse — Bertha lifted her head.
"Those weren't letters, sweetheart."
Her eyes met Jane's. Clear. Furious. Incandescently, terrifyingly lucid.
"Those were subpoenas."
The word landed in Jane's chest and detonated.

Subpoenas. Not pleas. Not prayers. Not the desperate scribblings of a madwoman hoping someone, anyone, might care. Legal instruments. Formal demands. Each stitched word a charge, each red-thread letter an article of indictment, sewn into the only medium she could control — fabric — sent out through the only channel she had — the garment rotation — addressed to anyone with eyes sharp enough to read what cloth was really saying.
And no one had. For five years. No one had read them.
Until Jane.

The room reconfigured itself around her. The silk-lined walls, which had seemed luxurious, now looked obscene — a gilded cage, the silk a softener, an anesthetic, another layer of control. The fabric on the shelves, the tools, the worktable — not comforts. Not amenities. Distractions. Pacifiers. The trappings of a life made tolerable enough to endure, because a prisoner who could work was a prisoner who wouldn't die, and a dead wife was far less convenient than a living one locked away where no one could hear her.
Jane turned to the racks of garments.

And now she saw them — really saw them, the way you see a pattern only after the frame changes. A deconstructed blazer with the interfacing exposed, the structure revealed, the hidden architecture of tailoring turned inside out and made into ornament. A dress made from three different garments, their seams mismatched and reconverged, a Frankenstein of beauty that shouldn't have worked but sang — a visual thesis on reclamation, on taking the broken and making it speak. A coat with the lining on the outside, the shameless luxury of silk charmeuse worn as armor.

They were brilliant. More inventive, more alive, more fearless than anything Thornfield produced under Edward's name. Because they were free — created without his approval, without his vision, without the Rochester brand ironed into every seam. Bertha Mason had been designing in secret for five years, alone, with whatever materials came through that door, and the work was a revolution. Every safety pin was a refusal. Every exposed seam was a confession. Every piece was a document — evidence, testimony, a record of a mind that refused to be broken even as everything around it tried.
The room was a prison. The work was a war.

Jane's hands were shaking. She pressed them against her thighs and made herself look back at Bertha, who was watching her with the still, patient attention of someone who had seen this moment play out in her head a thousand times and was now comparing the reality against the simulation.
"You designed all of this."
It wasn't a question. Bertha didn't answer it. She didn't need to.

Jane moved along the rack, fingers hovering over the garments without touching — she wouldn't touch them without permission, not these, not ever — reading each piece the way she'd read the seam allowances, the way she'd read every garment that passed through Thornfield's atelier. But these were different. These weren't made to be worn. They were made to be witnessed. Each one was a statement, a chapter, an argument constructed in cloth and thread, and collectively they told a story that no court in the world could dismiss.

"Five years," Bertha said. Her voice had found its register now — low, controlled, each word placed with the precision of a seamstress setting a stitch. "Five years of sending them out. In the linings. In the facings. In the hems. Every time Grace brings new material and takes away the finished pieces. I stitch the words in, she carries them out, they go into the Thornfield production line, and—" She stopped. The corner of her mouth twitched — not a smile, something harder. "And they end up in a landfill, or a discount rack, or some woman's closet in Lyon who never thinks to turn the jacket inside out and look at the seams."
"I looked," Jane said.
"You looked." Bertha repeated it like she was learning a new language. "You looked at the seams."
"I always look at the seams."

Something passed between them — a recognition that had nothing to do with the intimacy of strangers and everything to do with the intimacy of people who see the same hidden architecture in the world. The structure beneath the surface. The truth in the stitching.

Bertha stood. The trembling had stopped. Or rather, it had been contained — pressed down into some interior space where it could burn without consuming, fuel instead of fire. She came around the worktable, moving with an economy that Jane recognized from her own body: the careful, deliberate gait of someone who has learned that wasted motion in a small space is a kind of madness.
"How long do you have?"

Jane pulled out her phone. No signal — the basement's concrete and steel saw to that. But the clock worked. She calculated: the dinner service, the handoff to the night shift, the window before anyone noticed a body missing from the atelier.
"Maybe twenty minutes before they notice I'm gone."

Bertha absorbed this. Her eyes closed briefly — not in defeat, but in calculation. Twenty minutes. Five years of planning compressed into a sliver of time. Jane watched her decompose and recompose, priorities reordering themselves behind those dark eyes like a hand of cards being shuffled.

"Then listen carefully." Bertha stepped closer. Close enough that Jane could smell her — not the Turkish-bath sourness of Thornfield's mythology, but fabric softener and chalk dust and the faintly chemical sweetness of dye. Close enough to see the scar on her left temple, half-hidden by the brutal haircut. Close enough to count the lines around her eyes, each one a year. "Because I've had five years to plan, and you have twenty minutes to decide if you're going to help me end this."
She leaned forward. Her voice dropped. The silk walls seemed to lean in with her, the whole room contracting to the distance between two women and a truth too large for either of them to carry alone.
"He'll know you were here."
The words fell into the space between them like a stone into water, and the ripples they made were not the kind that faded.
"He always knows."

Jane felt the truth of it settle into her bones — the cold certainty that whatever came next would not be quiet, would not be gentle, would not be the slow unraveling she'd been performing for the past week. This was no longer a mystery to solve. This was a war to join. And the general had been waiting in a silk-lined room for five years, stitching subpoenas into the seams of beautiful things, for the one person who would think to look.
Twenty minutes. Five years. A door that only opened from outside.
Jane did not look at the door.
